◆ APM Module · UX Research & Architecture

Redesigning APM: Profiling & Dumps for Motadata ObserveOps

A product & UX blueprint that adds always-on code-level profiling and on-demand thread / heap / crash dumps to the ObserveOps APM module — grounded in the live Motadata docs and a deep read of the Datadog Continuous Profiler and Dynatrace Profiling & Optimization experiences.

Author roleSr. Product Designer / UX Architect / APM SME

SRC·2 docs.datadoghq.com · Continuous Profiler
SRC·3 docs.dynatrace.com · Profiling & Optimization

M
ObserveOps APMProfiling & Dumps · redesign

■ Datadog   ■ Dynatrace   ■ Motadata
P1 Critical P2 High P3 Med
Executive Summary

The one thing to build: a Profiling & Dumps pillar in APM

Motadata APM today is a strong trace-first product — its signature move is correlating a request's spans with host, JVM, network and DB metrics in one flame chart. But it stops at the request boundary. It has no way to answer "which line of my code is burning the CPU?" or "why did this JVM leak / hang / crash?" That is exactly the gap the attached spec targets, and exactly where Datadog and Dynatrace differentiate.

0
Code-level profilers in Motadata APM today (CPU / memory / lock)
3
Dump types competitors ship that Motadata lacks — thread, heap, crash
9
Span-level diagnostic tabs already in Motadata to reuse as profiling surfaces
P1
Priority of Profiling & Dumps in the gap analysis (§3)

The recommendation in five moves

  1. Add two new APM pillars — Profiling and Dumps — as first-class siblings of Services, Traces and Errors, not buried inside a span tab. Both are gated per-application by a simple on/off toggle in Settings → Application Registration, exactly as the spec sketches.
  2. Reuse the trace↔span↔host correlation Motadata already has as the connective tissue: every flame graph frame links back to the trace and endpoint it came from, and every slow span offers "Open in Profiling." This is Motadata's structural advantage — lean into it.
  3. Ship a single multi-pane Profiling Workspace (nav tree · flame graph · endpoint/insight inspector · thread timeline) instead of the 5-tabs-and-3-visualizations sprawl that makes Datadog and Dynatrace hard to learn.
  4. Do memory analysis in-product — dominator tree, leak suspects, allocation flame graph — the one place both competitors punt to external tools (Dynatrace exports to Eclipse MAT; that is a leapfrog opportunity).
  5. Let the AIOps engine drive: auto-flag CPU/memory regressions on deploy, auto-rank hotspots, and correlate a profiling anomaly to the same Problem/Alert object Motadata already uses — so profiling feeds RCA rather than being a separate silo.
Bottom line

Motadata does not need to out-feature Datadog. It needs the two capabilities its own docs are missing (continuous profiling + dumps), delivered through one calmer workspace that inherits Motadata's trace-to-infrastructure correlation. That combination is defensible, scoped, and directly buildable from the attached spec.

Deliverable 1 · Current State

How Motadata APM works today

Verified against the live documentation at docs.motadata.com/apm. One correction to the common assumption up-front: APM does not use a per-monitor tab bar of Overview · Transactions · API Endpoint · Database · JVM · Logs · Active Error. Those diagnostic tabs are real, but they live at the span level inside a trace — not at the service level.

Existing information architecture

APM (top-level module, alongside Log / Flow / RUM / SLO / Topology / Alerts) ├─ APM Explorer — real-time trace grid · toggle Trace View / Span View · Trace Count · Duration · Errors · Saved Views · Metric Insight ├─ Service detail │ ├─ Overview tab — Service Map · Requests&Errors · Response Time (P50/P99) · Error Count │ └─ Transactions tab — Trace Count/Duration/Errors · grid: Root Span · Traces · Spans · Duration · Errors ├─ Trace detail (flame chart) — request waterfall; per-span tabs ↓ │ └─ Info · Exception · JVM · Network · Host · Metric · Database · Process · Logs · Container ├─ Error Tracker — Exceptions / 5xx / 4xx trends · issue grid · stack traces · impacted traces ├─ Compare — two services side-by-side · Latency P99 / Throughput / Error-rate deltas · endpoint breakdown └─ Settings ▸ APM ▸ Application Registration — Java · .NET · PHP · Node.js · Python · Go (+ Auto/Custom instrumentation, OTEL agent)

Data model

Trace = the full journey of a request. Span = a step (DB call, API request, internal work). Collected agent-side over OpenTelemetry via the MotaAgent + otelcol. Java injected with -javaagent.

Signature strength

The trace flame chart lets you open any span and read its Host / JVM / Network / DB / Process metrics and ±15-min correlated logs in place. Few mid-market APMs stitch trace→infra this tightly.

Vocabulary in use

Latency / Response Time (P50/P95/P99), Throughput (req/min), Error Rate, Trace/Span Count, Root Span, Entities, Resource, Business Service. No Apdex in APM (RUM-only).

Today The documented "golden path" — investigating a latency + error spike
01
APM ExplorerSpot the error pattern on a service in the trace grid
02
Transactions tabAnalyse trend on the problematic root span
03
Trace listOpen traces for that root span
04
Flame chartInspect one trace's span waterfall
05
Span tabsInfo · Exception · Host — correlate to memory metric
06
Root cause"Excess host memory" — but no way to see which code allocated it

Current problems (redesign targets)

ProblemWhy it hurtsEvidence
No code-level profilerInvestigations dead-end at "the host was busy / out of memory." There is no CPU / wall-time / allocation profiling to name the method responsible.Not found in APM docs
Flame chart ≠ flame graphMotadata's "flame chart" is a per-request span waterfall, not a sampled CPU flame graph aggregated across many requests. The word collides; the capability doesn't exist.apm/view-analyze-traces
No thread / heap / crash dumpsDeadlocks, memory leaks and JVM crashes — the hardest production incidents — have no dedicated capture-and-analyse surface.Not found in docs
Diagnostic tabs buried at span levelJVM, Database, Host, Network live inside an individual trace's span. There's no service-scoped "JVM health" or "DB hotspots" view — you must first find a representative trace.apm/view-analyze-traces
No deployment / version trackingCompare is a manual two-panel diff. There are no release markers and no automatic "this deploy regressed CPU" detection.Not found in docs
Thin, per-service dependency mapA per-service Service Map exists, but no application-wide, click-to-traverse topology with request-flow direction.apm/overview
APM alerting undocumentedThe alert engine's only worked example is disk-space. No documented policy on APM latency / error-rate / profiling anomalies.alerts-and-policies
Heavy manual onboardingAgent install + otelcol running + -javaagent + per-app registration, with troubleshooting that assumes ingestion already works.apm/ingestion
Deliverable 2 · Competitor Analysis

Datadog & Dynatrace — how the leaders do profiling

Both were read at the documentation level and cross-referenced against the screenshots in the attached spec. The screenshots below are the exact evidence frames from that spec, annotated to your intended flow.

■ Datadog — Continuous Profiler (APM ▸ Profiles)

Datadog's model is service-first: you pick a service, expand it to its processes, then open a profile. The profiler captures a rich per-language set — CPU Time, Wall Time, Allocations / Allocated Memory, Heap Live Memory, Lock/Monitor contention, Exceptions, File & Socket I/O — and renders each as a flame graph with four sibling visualizations.

▲ Worth stealing
  • Connect traces ↔ profiles. From a slow span, "Open in Profiling" carries context into a full flame graph; a Focus-On selector scopes it to Span & children / Span only / Span time period / Full profile.
  • Differential flame graph with a 4-colour legend (red=slower, green=faster, purple=baseline-only, blue=new) — separates regression from restructure at a glance.
  • Endpoint-scoped profiling — filter a flame graph to one API endpoint to find the method slowing that route.
  • Version tag as a universal compare axis across traces, profiles, metrics and logs; deployment comparison sorts endpoints by % change.
▼ Where it's heavy
  • Tab sprawl: a profile has 5 tabs × 4 visualizations × a compare mode — a large mental model before first insight.
  • Config gated per language behind env vars (DD_PROFILING_TIMELINE_ENABLED, allocation flags) and version minimums; capability differs sharply by language.
  • Live-vs-indexed & retention load — users must track which dataset a screen shows and why a trace vanished.
  • Cost anxiety is in the IA — indexing filters and a "Costs" lens make pricing pressure visible everywhere.

■ Dynatrace — Profiling & Optimization (Classic)

Dynatrace's model is process-first: a continuous-CPU list of process groups, drilling to Method hotspots. Its hub has three tools — Continuous CPU profiling · Memory dumps · Process crashes. Notably, memory analysis is captured in-product but exported to Eclipse MAT / VisualVM / PerfView — a real gap.

▲ Worth stealing
  • Method hotspots layout: tabs for Top APIs · Execution time · Call hierarchy (forward) · Hotspots (reverse stacks) answer "what calls this?" and "what's actually hot?" from one dataset.
  • Filter inheritance: filtering Execution-time + Top-APIs automatically re-scopes Call hierarchy and Hotspots — one control drives every pane.
  • Execution-time breakdown separates on-CPU work from waiting: code · network I/O · disk I/O · lock · wait.
  • Consistent semantic colour — the same subsystem/API is the same colour across pie, area and code stacks.
  • Davis AI collapses hundreds of thousands of events into ~5 causally-linked Problems with ranked root cause.
▼ Where it's heavy
  • Dual Classic-vs-new IA: nearly every capability exists twice; Profiling & Optimization never migrated — users switch eras mid-investigation.
  • Memory analysis isn't in-product — dumps are captured then handed to external tools. No inline dominator tree or allocation flame graph.
  • Crash analysis is buried in a generic Events section on process/host pages.
  • Vocabulary tax: PurePath, Smartscape, Davis, Grail, DQL, process group, span kind — heavy proprietary lexicon.
  • Fragmented "code-level" story split between Continuous-CPU (Classic) and Services → Response-time (new).

Head-to-head — the capability matrix

CapabilityDatadogDynatraceMotadata (today)
Continuous CPU profilingFull CPU + Wall time, all langsFull process-group, 5 langsAbsent
Memory / allocation profilingFull alloc + live-heap flame graphCapture only exports to MATAbsent
Flame graph (code-level)Yes + timeline + call graphYes hotspots + call hierarchyNo (trace waterfall only)
Thread dump / timelineThread timelinePartialAbsent
Heap dump analysisLive-heap in profilerExport-onlyAbsent
Crash / core dumpsVia errorsProcess crashes signal + artifactsAbsent
Trace ↔ profile linkingBest-in-class Focus-On scopingResponse-time breakdownTrace↔host/JVM already strong
Deployment / version compareAutomatic version diff + endpointsDavis deploy eventsManual 2-panel Compare
Differential flame graph4-colour diffCompare viewsN/A
Dependency / topology mapService MapSmartscape 2-axisPer-service map
AI root-causeWatchdogDavis causalAIOps engine (alerts/correlation)
Ease of useSprawl + cost anxietyDual IA + jargonSimpler, but thin

Reading: Motadata's opportunity is the clear diagonal of Absent cells in the profiling/dumps rows — combined with the one place both leaders are weak (ease of use). Win by filling the gaps in a calmer UI, not by matching feature counts.

Deliverable 3 · Gap Analysis

Current → Competitor → Recommendation → Priority

Ordered by build priority. P1 items are the spec's core; P2 amplify Motadata's existing trace strength; P3 round out parity.

Current (Motadata)Competitor benchmarkRecommended improvementPriority
No CPU profilerDD CPU/Wall time · DT Continuous CPU profilingShip a continuous CPU + wall-time profiler with flame graph, gated by a per-app toggle. Entry via a new APM Explorer Profiling tab.P1
No memory profilingDD allocations + live-heap · DT export-onlyIn-product allocation flame graph + heap dominator tree + leak suspects. Leapfrog Dynatrace by never exporting.P1
No thread dumpsDD thread timelineOn-demand thread dump capture + a blocked/deadlock analyser and per-thread timeline.P1
No heap dumpsDT memory dumps (export)Heap dump trigger → dominator tree, biggest-retainers, class histogram, rendered inline.P1
No crash captureDT process crashes (signal + artifacts)Crash / core-dump capture with killing signal, crashing frame, downloadable artifacts, linked to the Alert.P2
Flame chart onlyDD flame graph · DT method hotspotsAdd method hotspots (Top APIs · Execution-time breakdown · Call hierarchy · reverse Hotspots) with filter inheritance.P1
Trace↔host onlyDD Focus-On trace↔profileExtend Motadata's existing span correlation with "Open in Profiling" + Focus-On scoping (span & children / span / period / full).P2
Manual CompareDD auto version diffDeployment markers + auto CPU/memory/error diff on deploy; differential flame graph (4-colour).P2
Per-service mapDT Smartscape · DD Service MapApp-wide dependency map with request-flow direction & health colouring; reuse platform Topology.P3
APM alerting unclearDD Watchdog · DT DavisFirst-class APM alert policies on latency / error-rate / profiling regression, feeding the existing Alert/Incident correlation.P2
Heavy onboardingDD/DT one-click enableGuided registration: auto-detect instrumentable apps, one toggle to enable profiling + dumps, live "traces flowing?" status.P3
No AI on codeDD Watchdog insights · DT DavisAI hotspot ranking + regression call-outs surfaced in the flame-graph inspector as "Insights."P3
Deliverable 4 · Information Architecture

The proposed APM module hierarchy

Profiling and Dumps become first-class pillars. Everything else keeps Motadata's existing names so the change is additive, not a re-learn. NEW = net-new · MOD = enhanced existing.

APM ├─ Overview — fleet health: services, error budget, top regressions, open Problems mod ├─ Services — catalog + per-service Overview / Transactions (unchanged) ├─ Traces & Spans — APM Explorer, flame chart, span diagnostic tabs (unchanged) ├─ Profiling new — the code-level pillar │ ├─ Explorer — continuous-profile entry · CPU chart (Line/Bar/Area) · process ⇄ service grid │ ├─ Flame Graph workspace — CPU · Wall · Allocations · Live-heap · Lock · I/O · Exceptions │ ├─ Method Hotspots — Top APIs · Execution-time · Call hierarchy · Hotspots │ ├─ Thread Timeline — per-thread lanes · waits · safepoints │ ├─ Compare — differential flame graph · version / period / baseline │ └─ Profiles & Insights — profile list · AI hotspot ranking · runtime info ├─ Dumps new — on-demand deep captures │ ├─ Thread dumps — capture · deadlock & blocked-thread analyser │ ├─ Heap dumps — dominator tree · leak suspects · class histogram (in-product) │ └─ Crash / core dumps — signal · crashing frame · artifacts ├─ Databases — service-scoped DB hotspots, promoted out of the span tab mod ├─ Errors — Error Tracker (unchanged) ├─ Dependencies — app-wide topology, request-flow direction mod ├─ Deployments new — release markers · version diff · faulty-deploy detection ├─ Alerts — APM policies inc. profiling regression → Alert/Incident correlation mod ├─ Dashboards — default + custom + drill-down └─ Settings ├─ Application Registration — per app: instrument · Profiling on/off · Dumps on/off mod └─ Profiling & Dumps config new — sample rate · retention · dump size caps · auto-capture rules
Placement rationale

Why two pillars, not one span tab? Profiling is aggregate & continuous (sampled across many requests); Dumps are point-in-time & on-demand. They have different data lifecycles, retention and cost profiles, so they deserve sibling nav — while both remain reachable in-context from a slow span via "Open in Profiling" / "Capture dump." This mirrors how Motadata already lets a span open Host/JVM tabs, just promoted to module level.

F7 Compact — the surrounding APM journeys

Error investigation

Error Tracker → issue → impacted traces → Exception tab (stack) → Open in Profiling to see the throwing hot path.

DB bottleneck

Databases view (promoted) → slow query → owning span → profile the calling method's wait time.

Alert-driven RCA

APM alert (latency/profiling regression) → correlated Incident → jump straight into the flame graph at the anomaly window.

Deliverable 6 · Screen Flow

How the screens connect

Every arrow is a real navigation. The design keeps three entry points into profiling (nav pillar, Explorer tab, and in-context from a span) so users reach it from wherever they already are.

APM Overviewfleet health · top regressions · open Problems
Services
↓

↓
▣ Flame-graph workspace
reached 3 ways → from a Span tab via "Open in Profiling" · from Profiling Explorer · from an Error's impacted trace
Method Hotspots→ Source code
Thread Timeline
Comparedifferential flame
Profiles & Insights
Dumpsvia "Capture dump"

Entry 1 · Nav pillar

Sidebar → Profiling. For proactive optimization work ("what's hot this week?").

Entry 2 · Explorer tab

A "Profiling / Continuous profile" tab beside Trace/Span view. Appears only when the app's toggle is on.

Entry 3 · In-context

From any slow span or error → "Open in Profiling" / "Capture dump," pre-scoped to that window. The reactive path.

Deliverable 7 · Navigation System

Sidebar · Header · Breadcrumb · Panels

A consistent frame across every APM screen so profiling never feels bolted-on.

◧ Left sidebar (persistent)

  • Module icons (existing 40–44px rail) → APM opens the pillar list: Overview · Services · Traces · Profiling · Dumps · Databases · Errors · Dependencies · Deployments · Alerts · Dashboards · Settings.
  • Within Profiling, a 222px context tree of applications → processes/services mirrors the RUM tree pattern already in the repo.

▤ Header

  • Global search / command palette (⌘K): jump to a service, endpoint, method, or "profile svc:store-backend."
  • Time-range pills 1h 24h 7d 30d + custom, shared across all panes.
  • Environment / version selector — the universal compare axis.

⛬ Breadcrumb

APM › Profiling › store-backend › process:web › CPU Time › InvokeAsync() — each hop clickable, so deep drill-downs stay reversible. Solves Motadata's "you must find a representative trace first" problem.

▦ Right inspector + bottom panel

  • Right inspector: selected-frame details, endpoint & function breakdown, AI Insights, "Open trace / Capture dump" actions.
  • Bottom panel: thread timeline / related traces / correlated logs — the master-detail move Motadata already uses for span logs.

⋮ Context menu (right-click, everywhere)

Open in ProfilingFocus on span & childrenCapture thread dumpCapture heap dumpCompare to baselineCompare to previous versionView sourceCreate analysis viewAdd to dashboardCreate alert on this metric
Deliverable 8 · Dashboard Strategy

Default, custom & drill-down dashboards

Default (ship-with)

  • APM Health — services, latency P50/P99, error budget, open Problems.
  • CPU Hotspots — top methods by self-CPU across the fleet.
  • Memory & GC — allocation rate, live-heap trend, GC pauses.
  • Deploy watch — regressions since last release.

Service & App dashboards

  • Per-service: RED metrics + profiling tab (CPU/mem by method) + recent dumps.
  • Per-app: process ⇄ service grid, top contributors, thread health.

Drill-down & compare

  • Any tile → underlying flame graph / trace list (drill-down).
  • Compare dashboards: two versions / two windows side-by-side, endpoints sorted by % change.
  • Widgets embeddable: flame-graph widget, hotspot top-list, dump inventory.
Principle

Dashboards are entry ramps, not dead ends. Every widget carries the click-through to the live analysis surface with the same time-range and filters intact — no re-navigation, no re-filtering.

Deliverable 9 · Multi-Pane Design

The Profiling Workspace — one screen, four coordinated panes

Datadog splits profiling across 5 tabs and 4 visualizations; Dynatrace splits it across two UI eras. Motadata's differentiator is consolidation: a single workspace where the panes are linked, so one action re-scopes everything.

Profiling Workspacestore-backend · CPU Time · 7d
◧ Left · Nav tree
apps → processes → services
▣ Center · Flame graph
WebServer.handle()
Rails.dispatch()datadog.trace()
AuthZ.check()render()middleware
pg.querycache
width = self-CPU share · darker = hotter · click a frame to focus
▦ Right · Inspector
Selected frame
Endpoints by cost
✦ AI Insight
▬ Bottom · Thread timeline / related traces / logs

Why this layout raises productivity

  • Filter inheritance (from Dynatrace): pick an endpoint in the right rail and the flame graph, hotspots and timeline all re-scope — one action, four panes.
  • Master-detail, no page loads (from Dynatrace's trace app): select a frame → inspector + bottom timeline update in place. You never lose your spot.
  • One colour language (from both): a subsystem is the same colour in the flame graph, timeline and trace waterfall — track it without reading labels.
  • Reactive + proactive in one frame: "Open in Profiling" drops you here pre-scoped; the same screen also serves open-ended "what's hot?" exploration.
Deliverable 10 · UX Recommendations

Enterprise UX principles applied

Navigation & reduced clicks

  • Promote JVM / DB / Host from span-only to service-scoped views — kill the "find a representative trace first" step.
  • Three entry points into profiling; command palette for zero-navigation jumps.

Search & filtering

  • Faceted sidebar (click) and query syntax (type) share one field — novices click, experts type.
  • Chart-to-data brushing: drag-select a CPU spike → the profile list filters to it.

Comparison & visualization

  • "Compare" is a verb on every profile, not a separate mode; 4-colour differential flame graph.
  • Group-by Function / Line / File / Directory; Line / Bar / Area toggles as in the spec.

Onboarding & troubleshooting

  • Auto-detect + one-toggle enable; a live "profiles flowing?" health strip.
  • Empty states teach: "No profile yet — generate load, or capture a manual profile."

Accessibility & honesty

  • Never rely on colour alone in the diff — pair red/green with ▲/▼ and % labels.
  • Live-vs-retained banners — be honest about sampling, like Datadog, so no one misreads a partial profile.

Enterprise scalability

  • Per-app sample-rate + retention + dump-size caps to control cost at fleet scale.
  • RBAC on dump capture (heap dumps can contain PII) with audit trail.

Deliverable 12 · Final Recommendation

Phased roadmap & the case for it

Build the spec in three releases, each shippable and each reinforcing Motadata's existing trace strength.

Phase 1 · Foundations
Continuous CPU profilingRegistration toggle · Explorer Profiling tab · process⇄service grid · CPU flame graph · method hotspots · source link. Ships the spec's core Dynatrace + Datadog path.
Phase 2 · Dumps + Memory
Dumps pillarThread dump + deadlock analyser · in-product heap dominator tree & leak suspects · crash capture. The leapfrog vs Dynatrace's export-only memory.
Phase 3 · AI + Compare
Deployments & intelligenceRelease markers · differential flame graph · AI hotspot ranking · profiling-regression alerts feeding the existing Incident correlation.

Every decision, justified

DecisionUX principle / competitor lesson
Two pillars (Profiling + Dumps), not a span tabDifferent data lifecycles & cost profiles; discoverability. Dynatrace burying crash analysis in Events is the anti-pattern to avoid.
Per-app on/off toggleDirectly from the spec; matches Datadog/Dynatrace enablement and controls cost/overhead at fleet scale.
Single consolidated workspaceBoth leaders fragment profiling (DD tabs, DT dual-IA). Consolidation is Motadata's usability wedge — the one axis where it can clearly win.
In-product memory analysisThe single feature both competitors do poorly (export-only). Highest differentiation-per-effort.
"Open in Profiling" from a spanExtends Motadata's existing trace↔host/JVM correlation — reuses a strength instead of rebuilding.
Profiling feeds Alerts/IncidentsKeeps AIOps the spine: a profiling regression becomes the same Problem object, not a new silo. Davis/Watchdog validate the pattern.
Segmented process⇄service gridReconciles Dynatrace (process-first) and Datadog (service-first) so users keep their mental model.
The recommendation, in one line

Add Profiling and Dumps as first-class APM pillars — CPU/memory/IO flame graphs + method hotspots + in-product thread/heap/crash dumps — surfaced through a per-app toggle and one consolidated multi-pane workspace that inherits Motadata's trace-to-infrastructure correlation and feeds its AIOps engine. Match the leaders' capability where it counts; beat them on calm.

Motadata ObserveOps · APM Module Redesign — Profiling & Dumps. Design proposal v1. Grounded in live docs (docs.motadata.com/apm), Datadog Continuous Profiler (docs.datadoghq.com/profiler), Dynatrace Profiling & Optimization (docs.dynatrace.com), and the "APM Profiling & dumps" spec. Competitor screenshots are the spec's own evidence frames. Wireframes are low-fidelity blueprints, not final UI.
